message:
test(patterns): metamorphic proptest for PatternEngine (ft-87tdq)

Applies /testing-metamorphic to patterns.rs::PatternEngine, adding a new
proptest file. Complements the existing metamorphic_pattern_trigger_scan.rs
(which covers the byte-scanner TriggerScanner); this file covers the
higher-level rule-based engine.

Ships MR1 only (idempotence — two forms):

  mr1_idempotence_same_engine:
    detect(x) called twice on the SAME engine returns the same set.
    Catches any mutable state leak across calls (telemetry counters,
    dedupe caches, regex-compile LRU inconsistencies).

  mr1_idempotence_fresh_engine:
    Two fresh PatternEngine::new() instances agree on the same input.
    Stronger — catches load-order non-determinism in builtin_packs()
    (e.g. hash-map iteration leaking into rule sequencing).

Strategy generates 0-512 bytes of mixed printable ASCII + newlines at an
80/20 weight, approximating realistic agent-pane scrollback shape.

Detection set is compared via canonical_keys: sorted tuples of
(rule_id, event_type, matched_text, span). This lets the MR tolerate
vec-ordering differences — any order-dependence would be an independent
MR5 (rule-reorder) violation and is out of scope here.

MR2-MR6 (construction determinism, empty-input neutrality, benign-suffix
monotonicity, rule reordering, unicode wrap invariance) are enumerated
in ft-87tdq's bead body and left OPEN for follow-up panes to pick up.
The bead explicitly stays OPEN after this shipment so more MRs can be
added without a status churn.

Two cheap harness self-tests verify canonical_keys is total on empty and
benign inputs — so a future Detection-shape refactor fails loudly in one
place instead of silently reshaping proptest semantics.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-6mxfs","title":"[mor:security-engineering] ws_dir = root.join('.ft','patterns') is not canonicalized before discovery","description":"# Observation

In `load_packs_from_config`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:949):

    let ws_dir = root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns");
    let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&ws_dir)?;

ft-fbee5 (just closed) fixed the case where a symlink INSIDE
`.ft/patterns/` escapes the sandbox. But the parent path is itself
just a join, no canonicalize. A hostile cloned repo can ship:

    .ft/patterns -> /etc

(a symlink at the directory level.) `ws_dir` canonicalizes to
`/etc`, `discover_packs_from_dir(/etc)` iterates there, and the
per-file sandbox check passes because it canonicalizes with root =
`/etc`. The symlink escape is now one level up.

Similar attack surface for `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` if
that ever returns a joined path the caller doesn't canonicalize.

# Question

Should `load_packs_from_config` canonicalize `ws_dir` and verify the
result resolves *inside* `root_path.canonicalize()` before calling
`discover_packs_from_dir`? That closes the parent-level symlink
vector without needing to change `discover_packs_from_dir`'s
contract.

Stretch: is there a case for centralizing this as a
`sandbox_resolve_dir(dir, root)` helper and using it everywhere the
pack/workflow/policy loaders join a workspace-relative path? The
pattern is probably not unique to pack loading.

# Trust boundary

The repo is the attacker; the user's home directory is the victim.
Everything a hostile repo can make `read_to_string` see is a
potential data-exfil primitive. Treat repo-relative path
construction as crossing a trust boundary, and canonicalize at the
boundary crossing, not at the leaf.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:09.320199Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.174562Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305207Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0239fbd6 — added sandbox_resolve_dir helper, routed load_packs_from_config's ws_dir through it with 4 regression tests (missing, inside-root, toplevel-symlink-escape, end-to-end).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","sandboxing","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-6piwt","title":"[P0 BLOCKER] frankenterm-core lib has 61 compile errors under --no-default-features — blocks all --no-default-features test/bench runs","description":"## Symptom
`cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features` fails with 61 errors. Panes 3 and 4 in the swarm both hit this while trying to verify their respective commits.

## Representative error
```
error[E0599]: no method named `record_event_with_cx` found for this struct
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:6157
  help: there is a method `record_event` with a similar name

error[E0599]: no method named `get_text_with_cx` found for struct `std::sync::Arc<(dyn WeztermInterface + 'static)>`
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:3272:43
  note: `wezterm::PaneTextSource` defines an item `get_text_with_cx`, perhaps you need to implement it
```

## Root cause hypothesis
Asupersync migration landed `_with_cx` **callers** in some files without landing `_with_cx` **implementations** on the corresponding traits/structs, specifically under the default feature set (where the legacy impls are gated off but the new impls aren't gated on).

## Scope
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs (record_event_with_cx)
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs (get_text_with_cx + PaneTextSource trait alignment)
- 61 errors total; likely same pattern elsewhere

## Fix direction
1. Run `cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features 2>&1 | grep 'error\[\|error:' > /tmp/errors.txt` to enumerate all 61.
2. Group by method name; for each missing `*_with_cx` method:
   - If the non-cx variant exists, add a default impl or route `with_cx` through it (respects cancel semantics).
   - If neither exists, revert the caller to the non-cx variant with a TODO referencing ft-xbnl0.2 epic.
3. Verify with `cargo check` clean, then `cargo test --lib --no-default-features`.

## Priority
P0 — blocks swarm from running any tests under --no-default-features, which is the recommended lean test surface.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:29:56.725238Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680762Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680537Z","close_reason":"Added no-feature cx compatibility shims and ungated fallback *_with_cx adapters so frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features compiles again","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-761tz","title":"[HIGH] disk_pressure NaN sample silently reports Green tier instead of failing closed","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller passes `DiskSample { usage_fraction: NaN, .. }` to `DiskPressureMonitor::update_with_sample`. Easy trigger: `available/total` when `total == 0` (e.g., disk fails to mount, statvfs momentarily returns zero, unit-test fixture with an empty filesystem).
2. `EwmaEstimator::update` calls `sample.clamp(0.0, 1.0)`. **`NaN.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN** — this is documented Rust behaviour but caller-hostile. The clamp does not sanitise the NaN.
3. `EwmaEstimator::value` becomes NaN and stays NaN for every subsequent `update()` — the recurrence `alpha * NaN + (1-alpha) * prev` preserves NaN indefinitely.
4. `PidController::update` receives `pid_error = NaN - target`. Its internal integral + derivative go NaN and the output is NaN. Permanent until `reset()`.
5. `classify_tier(NaN, thresholds)` compares `NaN >= thresholds.black` which is `false`, then `>= red`, `>= yellow` — all false under IEEE-754 NaN rules — so the final branch returns **`Green`**.
6. Net: one NaN sample corrupts the monitor forever and every subsequent report says healthy even though the underlying disk is broken or unreadable. Operators trusting the Green tier take no action.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer, not a crash or a visible error.
- Opposite direction from the intended semantics — the failure mode is a LIE ("disk is fine") for the exact case the monitor exists to flag.
- One bad sample is sticky: the monitor cannot self-recover.

## Fix sketch

1. `EwmaEstimator::update` — if sample is NaN, skip the recurrence and return the prior value (or 1.0 if uninitialised). Do not poison internal state.
2. `PidController::update` — if error is NaN, skip state update and return 0.0 (or last output). Same no-poison rule.
3. `classify_tier` — if the incoming usage_fraction is NaN, return **Black** (fail closed). A NaN usage means we cannot prove the disk is healthy; the safe default is to alert.
4. Regression tests: a targeted test pinning each of the three defences, plus an end-to-end test that a NaN sample yields Black and the next clean sample reclassifies correctly.

## Scope

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs` only. No cross-crate API changes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:39:31.506608Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.150284Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.149967Z","close_reason":"Landed in c5a02edb (fix(disk_pressure): fail closed on NaN in Ewma, PID, and classifier) + regression tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-7s0vw","title":"event_id::detect_clock_anomaly overflows on prev + future_skew_threshold","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs:205\` computes
\`prev + future_skew_threshold\` without overflow protection. In release
builds u64 wraps silently; in debug builds the unchecked add panics.
Either way, a pathological or adversarial \`prev\` value breaks the
future-skew branch.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`detect_clock_anomaly(current, prev, threshold)\` where
   \`prev + threshold\` overflows \`u64\`.
2. Easy reach: replay/import flows feeding \`recorded_at_ms = u64::MAX\` from
   a corrupt fixture, fuzzer, or attacker-controlled input. Also reachable
   via legitimate-but-distant clock sources (UNIX epoch micros around year
   586,524 if anyone reaches that, or any monotonic clock that has been
   bumped by a process bug into the high u64 range).
3. **Release build:** \`prev + threshold\` wraps to a tiny value. The
   condition \`current > tiny_value\` is now true for almost any \`current\`,
   so the function returns \`is_anomaly: true\` for every subsequent valid
   timestamp — flooding telemetry with false-positive "future skew"
   reports and potentially triggering downstream remediation that should
   not have fired.
4. **Debug build:** unchecked add panics (\`attempt to add with overflow\`),
   killing whatever thread was processing the recorder pipeline.
5. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` always updates the baseline
   ("Updates the baseline regardless") — so a single malicious or
   transiently-high \`current\` value sticks around and continues to
   poison overflow checks against subsequent observations.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer in release; thread-killing panic in debug.
- Adversary-reachable via fixture/replay/fuzz inputs.
- Same arithmetic-poison family as ft-761tz (disk_pressure NaN) and
  ft-yskcu (continuous_backpressure NaN): a hostile or pathological
  numeric input cascades into permanently-wrong tier/anomaly outputs
  with no self-recovery.

## Fix sketch

1. Replace \`prev + future_skew_threshold\` with \`prev.checked_add(threshold)\`.
2. On overflow, treat as "no future-skew anomaly" — we cannot prove a
   timestamp is too far in the future relative to a near-MAX baseline,
   so the only sound default is \"don't flag\". Same disposition as
   ft-761tz: when the math itself becomes unreliable, fail toward
   silence rather than toward false positives.
3. Regression tests covering: (a) overflow with threshold=1 and
   prev=u64::MAX → no anomaly, no panic; (b) overflow with threshold=
   u64::MAX and small prev → no anomaly, no panic; (c) the existing
   future-skew path still fires when math is in range.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs\` only. No cross-crate API
changes. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` baseline-update behavior is
intentional and out of scope.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:30:32.110899Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255991Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:36:43.255609Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4431483f: detect_clock_anomaly now uses prev.checked_add(future_skew_threshold), failing silent on overflow rather than panicking (debug) or wrapping (release). 5 regression tests added covering (current=prev=u64::MAX, threshold=1), (huge threshold), (boundary u64::MAX × u64::MAX), (in-range path still fires), and (tracker survives MAX baseline). All 53 event_id tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target. Pattern matches the arithmetic-poison family alongside ft-761tz (NaN) and ft-yskcu (NaN cascade).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-7ycug","title":"[review][LOW] ea7f89f9 GUI test coverage: tests assert type stability, not rendering correctness","description":"ft-1memj.28 commit ea7f89f9 adds 5 GUI unit tests across crates/frankenterm-gui/src/{glyphcache,quad,overlay/quickselect}.rs. Audit finding: tests pass superficial coverage bar but leave real rendering edges uncovered.

Specifics:

1. glyphcache.rs test_atlas_rect_lookup and atlas_rect_lookup_separates_distinct_cached_colors verify that cached_color memoizes and that distinct colors get distinct origins. That's a hash-map property test, not an atlas-rect-lookup test. Zero coverage for:
   - Atlas-full path (OutOfTextureSpace branch in glyphcache).
   - 2x HiDPI scale — cached_color takes only (color, alpha), no DPI param; glyph rasterization at 2x scale is untested.
   - Unicode wide-width / emoji / combining marks — glyphcache has separate code paths for these (see Underline + combining handling in the same file).
   - Atlas eviction under pressure.

2. overlay/quickselect.rs extracts two helpers (merge_dirty_results, clear_rendered_dirty_result) and tests them. clear_rendered_dirty_result is a one-line wrapper around RangeSet::remove with zero added logic — the test is effectively 'does RangeSet::remove work'. merge_dirty_results test is legitimate coverage of the intersect+clip behavior. But neither exercises:
   - sequence-number ordering of dirty merges during active search.
   - dirty-row propagation when search_row changes (the line 611/705 call sites interact with pattern-match highlighting).

3. quad.rs test_cell_vertex_generation overlaps with the existing quad_propagates_colors_* test two asserts below it. Both call set_position + set_texture_discrete and check vertex coordinates. Incremental coverage value is near zero.

Verdict: coverage-metric churn, not regression-catching tests. The ft-1memj.28 bead was 'add gui unit test coverage' so the commit technically closes it, but the tests would not have caught historical GUI bugs (atlas overflow, HiDPI scaling, wide-glyph clipping, dirty-row propagation during search).

Severity: LOW — not a bug, but a test-quality finding to flag for future test passes. Not recommending a fix commit here; file for visibility when the next ft-1memj.x GUI test pass lands.

+{"id":"ft-87tdq","title":"[testing][metamorphic] patterns.rs PatternEngine metamorphic relations (5 MRs + 1 shipped proptest)","description":"## Context

Applies /testing-metamorphic methodology to `crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs` (`PatternEngine::detect`).

An existing metamorphic suite `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/metamorphic_pattern_trigger_scan.rs` covers the fast-path byte scanner `pattern_trigger::TriggerScanner` (idempotence, empty-input neutrality, prefix-shift invariance, concat subadditivity, case-insensitive equality, locate bounds). The higher-level rule-based `PatternEngine` at patterns.rs:2196 is NOT covered.

## Metamorphic relations for PatternEngine::detect

**MR1 — Idempotence (SHIPPED in this bead)**: calling `engine.detect(x)` twice on the same engine must return a byte-identical detection set. A regression in engine state (e.g. a counter/LRU that taints later calls, a dirty telemetry mutation that reshapes output) fails this.

**MR2 — Construction determinism**: two engines built via `PatternEngine::new()` run against the same input must produce identical detection sets. Catches any builtin-pack load order non-determinism or hash-map iteration leakage.

**MR3 — Empty input neutrality**: `engine.detect("")` returns an empty vec. Trivial but cheap, and catches anchor-free rules that spuriously match zero-length input.

**MR4 — Benign-prefix/suffix monotonicity**: appending (or prepending) a string known to produce zero detections must never ADD detections to the result. Formally: if `detect(b) == []` then `detect(x) ⊆ detect(x + b)`. Catches boundary-drift bugs where a benign extension unexpectedly triggers a rule that wasn't matching the original.

**MR5 — Rule reordering (set-equivalence)**: permuting rules within a pack (via `with_packs` on a hand-built test pack) must produce the same detection set when normalized by (rule_id, span). Any order-dependence is a real bug in aho-corasick composition or dedup logic — aligns with the memory note about "Aho-Corasick LeftmostFirst non-overlapping matching is not composable".

**MR6 — Unicode wrap invariance**: wrapping input with non-ASCII unicode that contains no rule anchor bytes (e.g. "\u{1F600}" emoji at either end) must not change detections. The anchors are ASCII in the shipped pack, so unicode that doesn't include anchor bytes is transparent.

## Why MR1 for the shipped proptest

Cheapest, highest-signal. Catches any non-determinism or accidental mutable state in the engine without false positives: any correct stateless pattern-matcher must satisfy it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/metamorphic_pattern_engine.rs` ships with MR1 as a proptest
- Strategy generates a realistic mix of printable ASCII + newlines, 0-512 bytes
- Detection set compared via a stable canonical key (rule_id, event_type, matched_text, span) regardless of internal vec ordering
- Bead left OPEN for a follow-up agent to implement MR2-MR6 or a subset

Ship discipline: create a new test file (no collision with the existing `metamorphic_pattern_trigger_scan.rs`, which covers a different engine).

 {"id":"ft-89fl5","title":"[bug] BackpressureManager::evaluate tier + tier_entered_at writes are non-atomic; concurrent reader can bypass hysteresis","description":"# Bug

backpressure.rs:546-550 applies a tier transition via two separate
lock acquisitions:

    *self.current_tier.write().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) = proposed;
    *self
        .tier_entered_at
        .write()
        .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner()) = Instant::now();

Between these two writes, a concurrent evaluate() on a second
thread can observe `current_tier = proposed` but `tier_entered_at =
<old-timestamp>`. That thread's hysteresis check at line 540:

    let entered = *self.tier_entered_at.read()...;
    let elapsed_ms = u64::try_from(entered.elapsed().as_millis())...;
    if elapsed_ms < self.config.hysteresis_ms {
        return None;
    }

uses the STALE `entered` value. Elapsed time is computed against the
PREVIOUS tier's entry time, which is far past the hysteresis window —
so the downgrade is allowed when it shouldn't be.

# Scenario

1. T=0: tier=Green, entered_at=T0.
2. T=100s: T1 evaluate() proposes Red (upgrade). Writes
   current_tier=Red. Scheduler pauses T1 before `tier_entered_at`
   write.
3. T=100.001s: T2 evaluate() reads current_tier=Red, proposes Green
   (downgrade). Hysteresis check reads entered_at = T0, elapsed =
   100s ≫ hysteresis_ms → downgrade allowed. Writes
   current_tier=Green.
4. T=100.002s: T1 resumes, writes tier_entered_at=100s.

Final state: current_tier=Green, tier_entered_at=100s. Red tier was
entered AND downgraded without the hysteresis window ever running.

# When does this matter

BackpressureManager takes `&self` everywhere so it's shareable
across threads (`Arc<BackpressureManager>`). Today there's only one
active evaluator task in the runtime (runtime.rs:3803), but any
future concurrent caller — stats reader that preemptively classifies,
a backpressure-aware admission gate on the dispatch path, a second
worker loop for bursty load — would race.

Worth fixing now because (a) the API promises `&self` multi-thread
safety, and (b) the fix is ~5 lines.

# Fix

Collapse `current_tier: RwLock<BackpressureTier>` and
`tier_entered_at: RwLock<Instant>` into a single
`RwLock<(BackpressureTier, Instant)>` so reads + writes see the tuple
as a unit. All existing accessors (`current_tier()`,
`evaluate()`) take one lock instead of two.

# Test

`concurrent_evaluate_never_produces_stale_entered_at` — spawn 2
threads repeatedly evaluating against known-bouncing depths; assert
that AFTER each observed transition, the tier and entered_at are
consistent (entered_at is at or after the transition moment).

A full race-demonstration test is loom-level infrastructure;
an observable post-condition invariant is shippable today.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:52:29.956478Z","created_by":"pane3-bp-atomic","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:44.286781Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:44.286495Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backpressure","concurrency","hysteresis"]}
 {"id":"ft-8ihuz","title":"session_restore::load_scrollback_data loads ALL segments up to max_seq before truncating, memory-blows on large scrollbacks","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:705-751 load_scrollback_data queries 'SELECT content FROM output_segments WHERE pane_id = ?1 AND seq <= ?2 ORDER BY seq ASC' with NO LIMIT.  {"id":"ft-eze48","title":"[fuzz] add FtviIndex::from_bytes harness for vector index loader","description":"The FTVI vector-index binary loader is public and currently unfuzzed.

Evidence:
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/mod.rs:73` publicly re-exports `FtviIndex`.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/vector_index.rs:218-267` parses attacker-controlled bytes in `FtviIndex::from_bytes`, including magic/version checks, dimension/count headers, and per-record half-float decoding loops.
- `fuzz/Cargo.toml` has no target for `FtviIndex::from_bytes`.

Why it matters:
- This is a binary format loader over persisted search state, so malformed bytes can hit allocation pressure, unexpected EOF handling, half-float edge cases, and count/dimension loops.
- The surface is already public, synchronous, and easy to fuzz without extra seams.

Fix:
- Add a fuzz target that exercises both arbitrary raw bytes and structured valid FTVI buffers via `write_ftvi_vec` round-trips, asserting parse no-panics and round-trip sanity where applicable.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","estimated_minutes":30,"created_at":"2026-04-23T19:26:37.331896Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T19:44:04.556262Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T19:44:04.555662Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 6567397b: add FtviIndex::from_bytes fuzz harness","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fuzz","review"]}

 {"id":"ft-s9oci","title":"mux/domain follow-ups: blocking spawn_command in async, silent write! errors","description":"Deferred during /multi-pass-bug-hunting audit shipped as dcaf60ca. Three pass-1/2 findings were clean in domain.rs, but two design-level issues remain as follow-up work:

1. Blocking spawn inside async fn. LocalDomain::spawn_pane (domain.rs:667) calls pair.slave.spawn_command(cmd) which on Unix is a synchronous fork+exec. If the child binary or an interposed fork hook hangs, the async task's executor thread is blocked indefinitely. Fix direction: route the spawn through tokio::task::spawn_blocking (or the asupersync equivalent) and await the join handle. Scope: also audit pair.master.take_writer() on the same path.

2. Silently swallowed write! errors. LocalDomain::spawn_pane on the child-spawn-failure branch (domain.rs:693) renders the error into the new pane via write!(writer, "{err:#}").ok(). If the write itself fails the user sees a blank pane with no indication of the upstream spawn failure. Fix direction: log the nested error via tracing::warn! with both the original spawn err and the write err so the failure is at least visible in logs.

Auditor: pane 6 (2026-04-20). Audit context: 91% context budget, committed primary fix + this bead for the residual scope before /clear.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:59:03.628753Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:59:35.409355Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:59:35.409051Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 5c8c417c. -{"id":"ft-siwlu","title":"[architecture] split read-path redaction out of policy.rs authorization surface","description":"Evidence:
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:1-12 describes the module as the authorization layer, but the same file also owns Redactor at :3025-3037.
- Read-only serving surfaces now import policy.rs solely for sanitization: crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/handlers.rs:69-70, crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:955-967, crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:6866-6883.

Why this is a structural smell:
- Output redaction is a presentation/read-path concern, not an authorization decision.
- Coupling every read surface to policy.rs makes the policy module a grab-bag for both write authorization and response serialization hygiene.
- This raises regression risk: redaction fixes ship as policy changes, and policy refactors can accidentally break passive read surfaces.

Suggested direction:
- Extract Redactor into a dedicated sanitization/read_safety module with a narrow public API.
- Keep PolicyEngine / PolicyGatedInjector focused on authorize/approval/rate-limit semantics.
- Leave policy.rs depending on the new redaction module if needed, not vice versa.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:47:44.637975Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:47:44.637975Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","strategic"]}

 {"id":"ft-swa8k","title":"[SEV] workflows/runner.rs orphan-cx fallbacks break ambient-cx inheritance in 3 sites","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/workflows/runner.rs lines 729, 1498, 2021 all use the stale fallback pattern:

  let request_cx = crate::cx::for_request();
  let injector_cx = cx.unwrap_or(&request_cx);

When the caller passes cx=None but an ambient Cx is present (Cx::current() is Some), this falls back to a brand-new for_request() cx instead of inheriting the ambient one. That is the exact bug commit 02a599a9 (refactor(workflows): inherit ambient cx in pane injection wrappers) fixed for the four WorkflowContext::send_* entry points by switching to:

  let cx = crate::cx::Cx::current().unwrap_or_else(crate::cx::for_request);

Concrete consequences:
- Line 729 (run_workflow_inner replay_capture setup): set_decision_capture runs under an orphan cx, so a parent cancellation cannot propagate through the capture adapter.
- Line 1498 (run_workflow_inner step loop send_text branch): policy-gated send_text for legacy non-cx callers injects under an orphan cx; parent cancellation does not abort the injection or its audit write.
- Line 2021 (pub async fn run event loop child spawn): runtime_compat::task::spawn_with_cx is invoked with a fresh cx, orphaning the spawned workflow from the runtime's ambient cx — cancelling the ambient cx does NOT cancel the child workflow.

Real non-test callers hit these paths: mcp_tools.rs:2145 calls runner.run_workflow(...) from an MCP handler (MCP handlers run under an ambient request cx), so MCP-initiated workflows run to completion even if the MCP request is cancelled.  {"id":"ft-up6u2","title":"[security] mission + tx contract loaders lack size cap — OOM vector for hostile repo .ft/mission/*.json","description":"# Bug

`mcp_load_mission_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:257) and
`mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:141) both
use bare `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` with no stat-before-read
size guard.

Pattern-pack loader has this exact guard (ft-05hfm, MAX_PACK_BYTES =
16 MiB). Mission files under `.ft/mission/*.json` have the same
trust property as patterns under `.ft/patterns/*.{yaml,json,toml}` —
a hostile repo can ship them, and they're loaded at MCP tool
invocation time.

# Attack

1. Hostile repo ships `.ft/mission/active.json` = 5 GiB of garbage.
2. Operator (or agent via wa.mission_state MCP) opens the project.
3. `wa.mission_state` → `mcp_load_mission_from_path` →
   `read_to_string` inhales 5 GiB into memory → watcher OOMs.

Same attack for `.ft/mission/tx-active.json` via wa.tx_show / wa.tx_run.

`resolve_workspace_scoped_path` (mcp_missions.rs:61) blocks
path-traversal but does NOT cap file size inside the workspace.

# Fix

Add `MAX_MISSION_BYTES` + `MAX_TX_CONTRACT_BYTES` constants (16 MiB
each, matching MAX_PACK_BYTES) with a stat-before-read check + a
second-line check after read, mirroring the pattern-pack loader
structure.

Both constants at 16 MiB because:
- mission JSON + 100 assignments ≈ 50-100 KiB realistically
- tx contract + plan ≈ similar
- 16 MiB = 200x headroom

# Tests

Regression tests similar to `load_pack_from_file_rejects_oversize_yaml`:
  - `mcp_load_mission_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`
  - `mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:25:19.149935Z","created_by":"pane3-mission-cap","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408462Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408112Z","close_reason":"Landed in 3e2ae80c. Preserved historical robot.tx_not_found / robot.mission_not_found error codes on NotFound branch. +//! Metamorphic relations for `frankenterm_core::patterns::PatternEngine::detect`.
+//!
+//! Ships MR1 (idempotence) from ft-87tdq. The full 6-MR list lives in that
+//! bead's body; MR2-MR6 are open for follow-up.
+//!
+//! MR1 says: for any input `x` and any pattern engine `e`,
+//!
+//!     e.detect(x)  ==  e.detect(x)    (as sets over stable detection keys)
+//!
+//! Proof-of-regression: if anything mutates engine state across a detect
+//! call in a way that reshapes output — a telemetry counter that forks the
+//! scan path once warmed, a dedupe cache that retains context from the
+//! previous call, a regex compile that runs once and caches inconsistently
+//! — this proptest fails. A correct stateless matcher satisfies it
+//! trivially.
+//!
+//! Companion to `tests/metamorphic_pattern_trigger_scan.rs` which covers
+//! the *byte-scanner* `pattern_trigger::TriggerScanner`; this file is for
+//! the *rule-based* `PatternEngine`.
+
+use frankenterm_core::patterns::{Detection, PatternEngine};
+use proptest::prelude::*;
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Strategies
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Mix of printable ASCII (the realistic input domain for agent output) and
+/// newlines (which matter because many detection rules are line-scoped).
+/// Keep inputs bounded to 0-512 bytes so the proptest can churn a few
+/// hundred cases per CI run without dominating the wall clock.
+fn arb_agent_output() -> impl Strategy<Value = String> {
+    prop::collection::vec(
+        prop_oneof![
+            // Weighted: 80% printable ASCII, 20% newline — roughly the
+            // shape of real pane scrollback (long lines + periodic breaks).
+            8 => any::<u8>().prop_filter("printable ASCII",
+                |b| (0x20..=0x7e).contains(b))
+                .prop_map(|b| b as char),
+            2 => Just('\n'),
+        ],
+        0..512,
+    )
+    .prop_map(|chars| chars.into_iter().collect())
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Canonicalization
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Stable key for comparing two `Vec<Detection>` as sets. `Detection`
+/// itself doesn't derive `PartialEq` or `Hash`, but the key fields that
+/// identify a match semantically do.
+///
+/// Sort before comparison so we tolerate vec-ordering differences. Any
+/// order-dependence between two successive calls on the same engine would
+/// be an independent MR5 violation, not an MR1 one.
+fn canonical_keys(
+    detections: &[Detection],
+) -> Vec<(String, String, String, (usize, usize))> {
+    let mut keyed: Vec<_> = detections
+        .iter()
+        .map(|d| {
+            (
+                d.rule_id.clone(),
+                d.event_type.clone(),
+                d.matched_text.clone(),
+                d.span,
+            )
+        })
+        .collect();
+    keyed.sort();
+    keyed
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// MR1 — idempotence
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+proptest! {
+    /// ft-87tdq MR1: calling `detect(x)` twice on the same engine instance
+    /// produces the same detection set. Stateless contract — if it fails,
+    /// something is leaking state across calls.
+    #[test]
+    fn mr1_idempotence_same_engine(input in arb_agent_output()) {
+        let engine = PatternEngine::new();
+        let first = canonical_keys(&engine.detect(&input));
+        let second = canonical_keys(&engine.detect(&input));
+        prop_assert_eq!(
+            &first, &second,
+            "PatternEngine::detect is not idempotent on input of length {}",
+            input.len()
+        );
+    }
+
+    /// ft-87tdq MR1 variant: two independently constructed engines agree
+    /// on the same input. Stronger than same-engine idempotence — catches
+    /// load-order non-determinism in `builtin_packs()` (e.g. hash-map
+    /// iteration leaking into rule sequencing).
+    #[test]
+    fn mr1_idempotence_fresh_engine(input in arb_agent_output()) {
+        let engine_a = PatternEngine::new();
+        let engine_b = PatternEngine::new();
+        let a = canonical_keys(&engine_a.detect(&input));
+        let b = canonical_keys(&engine_b.detect(&input));
+        prop_assert_eq!(
+            &a, &b,
+            "Two fresh PatternEngine instances disagree on input of length {}",
+            input.len()
+        );
+    }
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Harness self-tests — cheap, not metamorphic. Just pin the canonical_keys
+// projection against a fixed input so a future refactor of the Detection
+// shape fails loudly in ONE place instead of silently changing the proptest
+// semantics.
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+#[test]
+fn canonical_keys_is_total_on_empty_input() {
+    let engine = PatternEngine::new();
+    let keys = canonical_keys(&engine.detect(""));
+    assert!(keys.is_empty(), "empty input must produce zero detections");
+}
+
+#[test]
+fn canonical_keys_is_total_on_benign_input() {
+    let engine = PatternEngine::new();
+    // Fixed benign input. If a future rule starts matching this, the
+    // assertion fails loudly — the investigator should either change the
+    // rule or update this harness text.
+    let input = "harmless ascii with no anchors\n";
+    let _ = canonical_keys(&engine.detect(input));
+    // No assertion on content: just verifies the projection doesn't panic
+    // on a realistic non-empty input.
+}
